rewindjs.com
Quickstart

From a function to a URL.

A rewind.js app is a small bundle: one handler module, plus any static files. You publish the bundle, flip it live, and it serves. Here's the whole shape.

Pre-launch. Hosted sign-up isn't open yet, so there's no npm i step to run today. This page is the conceptual anatomy — the handler contract and the deploy lifecycle — so the pieces are familiar when the door opens.

1. The bundle

A tenant's deploy bundle is a directory. Two conventions matter:

myapp/
  index.mjs        // the handler — default export = inbound HTTP
  _static/         // static assets, served at their path sans _static/
    style.css
    logo.svg

Everything under _static/ is served as a static asset. Documents are served inline same-origin; other assets resolve to immutable, content-addressed URLs that cache forever. Your index.mjs is compiled to bytecode at publish time.

2. The handler

The default export runs on every inbound request. It's a pure function of the request global and the tenant's kv; it returns the response body.

// index.mjs
export default function () {
  if (request.method === "POST") {
    kv.set("note/" + Date.now(), request.body);
    response.status = 201;
    return "saved";
  }
  const notes = kv.prefix("note/", "", 100);
  return { count: notes.length, notes };
}

The request surface (method, path, headers, query, body) and the ambient response head (status, headers, cookies) are covered in Handlers.

3. Publish → release → serve

Deployment is two phases, so a build is never half-live:

  1. Publish. The bundle is uploaded; sources compile to bytecode and assets are stored content-addressed. This produces an immutable deployment with an id — staged, not yet serving.
  2. Release. A single atomic flip points the tenant at that deployment id. The next request serves the new code; in-flight requests finish on the old one. Rolling back is the same flip to a prior id.
Releases commit through the same replicated log as your data, so "it's live" means a quorum agreed it's live — not that one machine hopes so.

4. It serves

Your tenant answers on its host. A request flows: edge TLS → router → your handler (a fresh, deterministic activation) → response. Every external read along the way is taped, so that exact request can be replayed later.

$ curl https://myapp.example/         # GET → your default export
{"count":0,"notes":[]}

Next

You've seen the shape. The reference goes deeper: Handlers for the full activation surface, and Effects & replay for how outbound work stays deterministic.
